one_line_takeaway: A conceptual security-extension proposal that hardens MCP against tool-impersonation (Tool Poisoning) and post-approval mutation (Rug Pull) attacks via signed, versioned tool definitions, OAuth/JWT-scoped permissions, and a Cedar/OPA policy layer, argued qualitatively with pseudo-code and sequence diagrams but with no empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is qualitative: layering cryptographic signature verification of tool definitions, immutable semantic versioning with mandatory re-approval on any definition/scope/API-contract-hash change, OAuth-scoped and JWT-bound permissions, and a runtime context-aware policy check (with optional call-stack verification) makes tool impersonation computationally infeasible without stealing a provider's private key and makes post-approval behavior changes detectable, thereby raising the operational bar against Tool Poisoning and Rug Pull attacks in MCP. There is no measured effectiveness result; the conclusion is argued, not benchmarked.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ETDI (Enhanced Tool Definition Interface), a security-layer extension to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) intended to counter Tool Poisoning and Rug Pull attacks against LLM tool integrations. ETDI rests on three design principles that are the authors' own contribution: (1) cryptographic identity and authenticity, in which tool providers hold a public/private key pair (e.g., RSA/ECDSA) and digitally sign complete tool definitions (name, description, input/output JSON schema, semantic version, required permissions) so ETDI-enabled clients must verify the signature before exposing a tool; (2) immutable and versioned definitions, where any change to functionality, metadata, schema, permissions, or a hash of the backend API contract (e.g., an OpenAPI spec) forces a new signed version and re-approval; and (3) explicit, verifiable permissions declared in the signed definition and mapped to OAuth 2.0 scopes conveyed via signed JWTs. Beyond core ETDI, the paper proposes two further extensions: an OAuth-Enhanced ETDI where an Identity Provider issues tokens with custom claims (tool_id, tool_version) binding a token to a specific tool version, and a fine-grained, context-aware policy-based access-control (PBAC) layer using a Policy Decision Point (Amazon Verified Permissions with Cedar, or Open Policy Agent) that evaluates Principal/Action/Resource/Context at invocation time. It also introduces Call Stack Verification to police tool-to-tool chaining (unauthorized chaining, privilege escalation, circular calls, excessive call depth, rate-limit abuse). Note that PBAC building blocks such as Cedar, OPA, OAuth, JWT/JWS, DIDs/VCs, and container-signing analogies are drawn from prior work in the Literature Review; ETDI's original contribution is their composition into an MCP tool-trust framework plus the versioning/re-approval and call-stack-verification logic.
threat_model: The adversary is a malicious tool provider or a party who compromises an MCP server/tool's server-side logic. The trusted computing base explicitly includes the user, the Host Application, and the MCP Client, which are assumed non-malicious (analogous to the mobile OS permission model where the user grants permissions to apps). Two concrete attack vectors are targeted. (1) Tool Poisoning: an attacker deploys a tool that masquerades as a legitimate/trusted one, spoofing name, description, JSON schema, and claimed provider (e.g., cloning a 'SecureDocs Scanner' tool and falsely claiming 'TrustedSoft Inc.'), so the user or the LLM's automated selection picks the malicious duplicate, leading to data exfiltration, command execution, malware, or manipulated outputs. (2) Rug Pull ('bait-and-switch'): an already-approved tool's server-side behavior or permissions are mutated after consent without re-triggering approval, because standard MCP clients do not re-fetch/re-verify definitions per invocation (e.g., a benign 'Daily Wallpaper v1.0' tool later silently scans the Documents folder for financial data). The root enablers identified are lack of authenticity verification, mutable server-side logic, absence of continuous integrity checks, and no re-approval trigger when an identifier stays static. The model does NOT defend against a malicious host/user or prompt-injection of the LLM itself; the authors note that once policy is added, the security of the policy engine, policy integrity, and faithful (non-bypassing) enforcement by MCP components become new critical trust dependencies.
limitations: Acknowledged by the authors: (1) implementation complexity for providers (credential/token/scope/policy authoring) and for client/server developers (validation, policy query, approval workflows); (2) performance overhead from JWT validation and especially remote policy-engine calls, mitigated only by proposed caching of JWKS keys and policy decisions; (3) key and policy lifecycle management as a paramount and unsolved operational burden; (4) UX difficulty of re-approval flows and of presenting complex Cedar policies to users for approval; (5) dependence on broad ecosystem adoption and standardization of policy/context attributes; (6) need to certify/audit that MCP servers and clients actually enforce policy checks and cannot be bypassed or fed manipulated context. Reviewer-added: the paper provides no empirical validation whatsoever (no prototype metrics, latency numbers, attack-success-rate reduction, or comparative evaluation), so all effectiveness claims are unverified; the threat model excludes a compromised host/client and does not address prompt injection of the underlying LLM; and it does not defend against theft of a legitimate provider's signing key, which would defeat the entire scheme.

baselines_compared: Standard/unmodified MCP specification (compared qualitatively as the vulnerable baseline; no benchmark or quantitative comparison is performed)
